text.title: Error Prevention — Nielsen Heuristic #5 Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Error Prevention — Nielsen Heuristic #5 application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every error class MUST be evaluated against all six prevention strategies. Skipping strategies is rejected.
rule.rationale: Picking the first strategy that fits produces shallow prevention; evaluating all six surfaces better matches.

rule.statement: Errors MUST be ranked by frequency × cost. Unranked audits are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without ranking the team fixes the easy errors; the expensive ones rot.

rule.statement: Destructive actions MUST always be paired with confirmation OR be undoable. No-confirmation no-undo destructive actions are a hard fail.
rule.rationale: Destructive actions without confirmation or undo are the most common trust-killer; this rule is non-negotiable.

rule.statement: Defaults MUST favour the user's most likely intent, not the business's preferred outcome (e.g., do not default-opt-in to marketing email).
rule.rationale: Defaults are powerful; dark-pattern defaults erode trust and create regulatory exposure.

rule.statement: Each implemented prevention MUST be tested (analytics, usability test, or A/B) to confirm error-rate reduction. Untested preventions are rejected as complete.
rule.rationale: Implementation is not validation; the audit closes only when the error rate actually drops.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
